title: Why this ex-Liberal thinks she can unite One Nation and Greens voters
text:
The idea is being praised by some and ridiculed by others, but Charlotte Mortlock is convinced a new political party is just what Australia needs right now.

"If you can't have a crack in Australia, where can you have a crack?" the former political staffer tells triple j hack.

"I am not naive, I am ambitious. Australia can do hard things."

Promising to "reinvent the wheel" and support "Australians who are feeling politically abandoned", Mortlock says her Something Better campaign is focused on capitalising on the common interests of Australians, rather than their differences.

Loading...

"So many Australians aren't happy with the establishment, and they think that there are a lot of systemic flaws in our society," she says.

"The problem that we have with the major parties is they have refused to pick up on that sentiment."

Mortlock says she hopes the grassroots campaign will lead to the establishment of Australia's "next major political party".

"We are in a unique political landscape at the moment that is ripe for disruption," she says.

" We either let the disruption go the way that it continues to go … or we try something new. "

'No hope' for Liberals

A few months ago, Mortlock herself might have aligned with those laughing off her dream.

A strong advocate for the Liberal Party for years, the former journalist was well-known as the founder of Hilma's Network, a national movement encouraging more women to join and shape the Liberal Party.

Opposition Leader Angus Taylor (centre) at a press conference at Parliament House in Canberra. (ABC News: Callum Flinn)

But in February Mortlock quit the Liberal Party and the group she set up, in protest at the direction the party was heading.

She says the decision felt inevitable after the party toppled its first female leader, Sussan Ley.

"It was time to accept that they weren't going to change," she tells hack.

" I could no longer, in good faith, keep putting my energy towards it and also to keep encouraging other people to join a movement that was starting to feel like it actually wasn't aligning with me anymore. "

While acknowledging that she thinks Ley "made mistakes" as party leader, Mortlock maintains the former member for Farrer was not given a fair chance.

"The second that she got up, they just circled her like sharks."

"They", according to Mortlock, were Liberal MPs with "ambitions for themselves and for their own factions", who she says "undermined and rattled" the former opposition leader.

The Liberal Party's decision to oust Sussan Ley as leader was one of the reasons Charlotte Mortlock decided to leave the party. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

Mortlock says the move against Ley was a mistake, pointing to research showing the number of women voting for the Liberal Party has been dropping since 2004.

"There is no resentment from me … I just don't have hope for the Liberal Party, and I don't think it can evolve."

Learning from One Nation

Mortlock says the idea for Something Better came after noticing a rise in "anti-establishment" rhetoric in Australia, which has led many voters to turn away from the major parties in favour of independents and One Nation.

"[People] are working really hard and they aren't getting ahead," she says.

"They just genuinely want change and they don't think the major parties are going to be able to deliver it.

"I think people want a political party that is radical, but not necessarily in ideology. They want it to be radical in ambition and in tenacity."

Charlotte Mortlock was inspired by people turning away from major parties and towards independents and Pauline Hanson's One Nation. (ABC News: Che Chorley)

Asked where the party she's hoping to kickstart will sit on the political spectrum, Mortlock says it will be more issues-based than ideologically bound.

"We've got to get away from this left versus right, conservative versus progressive discussion," she tells triple j hack.

"There are so many policies that I think could be unifying for everyone from the Greens to One Nation.

"Is it left or right, or is it just common sense?"

Uniting Australians

According to Mortlock, Something Better will focus on long-term goals and a vision for Australia, rather than election promises.

She is convinced her policies will have broad appeal, citing high-speed rail as an issue that galvanises support across the country.

But she is quick to point out that she will not be running her own agenda.

"This is a movement that I'm starting, but over my dead body am I having the arrogance to say what I think a policy should be," she says.

Loading Instagram content

"We will be hosting events that have panellists that pitch different ideas and hopefully really creative, bold policies.

" We listen to experts, we listen to evidence, and we have the right people talking to policies. "

With hundreds signing up to the campaign within 24 hours of launching, Mortlock says it is clear the idea is worth pursuing.

Eventually she is hoping the movement will launch an official political party, which can only be registered once at least 1,500 members are signed up, or at least one senator or member of the House of Representatives comes on board.

'It's not the teal movement'

She is dismissing criticism that her idea is just a rebrand of the existing Teal movement.

"The Teals are independents," she says.

"No matter how effective an individual is, I think it would be really unifying to create a new party that is a collective movement.

"If you get really valuable candidates who win a seat, you want them in cabinet, you don't want them scattered on the crossbench."

'Why wouldn't I?'

While the policies remain undecided, the one thing Mortlock is sure of is that she will not be running for parliament herself.

"I don't want to be a politician," she stresses.

"I would like to work for the new party … but I'm definitely not running myself."

Charlotte Mortlock tells hack her new political movement is about giving Australians another option. (triple j hack)

She's putting the call out to anyone who is interested in change, including current independent MPs.

Mortlock says anyone is welcome to join the movement, regardless of their political beliefs.

"Yes, I am a former Liberal, but I am happy to make compromises on various things.

" I really think that there is more common ground than there is stuff to disagree about. "

She is also dismissing those who claim that she is in over her head, or should give up politics altogether.

"We have to take personal responsibility for things that we want in our society, not complain," she says.

"Why wouldn't I have a crack at this?

"I am resilient enough to withstand a bit of public humiliation for the risk of creating something that could be really incredible."

title: Family channels grief into thousands of dollars for Leukaemia Foundation
text:
This Mother's Day is the sixth year Christine Gray's family has spent without their cherished Mum and Nanny.

"As a mum, she was more our best friend," eldest daughter Jodie Collins said.

In 2018, Christine and husband George Gray were away on a camping trip with their four daughters — Jodie Collins, Holly Sedgwick, Elli Wust and Emily Gray — their partners and four grandchildren.

Elli Wust capturing a family moment in the pool of Nanny with her daughters and grandchildren. (Supplied: Elli Wust)

When Christine woke one morning with a sore shoulder, the 56-year-old thought she had slept on it wrong, eldest daughter Jodie recalled.

When the pain increased, George took her to hospital and after many tests, Christine was diagnosed with multiple myeloma, a rare blood cancer.

"Once they did a full-body scan, she actually had it in her skull," Jodie said.

Christine Gray went through two years of treatment battling the rare blood cancer, multiple myeloma. (Supplied: Elli Wust)

"I think it was eight ribs, her femur, in her shoulder … It was throughout her body."

Christine had two years of radiation, chemotherapy and a stem-cell transplant.

In 2020, she was admitted to the Intensive Care Unit after a fall and later developed sepsis, which led to her death.

Memories of Mum

Amid the grief in the following months, the sisters and their father talked about "something good" arising from Christine's death.

"I think the first words we said were, like, memories of Mum, something like that, and it's just stuck since and we thought we'd do an event," Elli said.

"It started the next year. It was in my backyard with about 30 people."

The Memories of Mum event is now in its sixth year and has evolved to a black-tie occasion.

Sisters Elli Wust, Emily Gray, Jodie Collins and Holly Sedgwick at the Memories of Mum event in 2023. (Supplied: Elli Wust)

This year they held two events: One in Rockhampton, where Elli and Jodie live, and one in Tamborine on the Gold Coast, where the family grew up and George and extended family live.

"The core value of the event is celebrating mums that aren't with us, but also celebrating the ones that are," Elli said.

Since Christine was diagnosed, the family has raised $72,000 for the Leukaemia Foundation, with just shy of $50,000 from the Memories of Mum events alone.

"We don't actually think too much about the money itself, we love raising it, it's just a bonus because we're creating such a nice platform," Elli said.

The donated money supports families like the Gray's, Leukaemia Foundation head of research Bill Stavreski says.

Christine Gray, pictured with husband George and granddaughter Harper, tried to spend as much time as possible with her family, even during treatment. (Supplied: Elli Wust)

"That includes providing accommodation free of charge for families who need to move to be closer to treatment … financial support, transport," he said.

"We fund research to be able to not only discover new treatments, but also to provide better diagnostics and ongoing care and support."

More than 170,000 people are living with blood cancer or a blood-related disorder in Australia, and 17 people die each day, according to the foundation.

Described as a silent and hidden cancer, it is the second most common diagnosed in women in Australia, behind breast cancer.

Christine's symptom of a sore shoulder was typical for blood cancer, Bill Straveski said, as the signs and symptoms could be quite vague.

"It can be anything from soreness, feeling lethargic, ongoing infections or temperature, elevated temperatures: they can mimic many other conditions, even the common cold and flu," he said.

Christine Gray, pictured with granddaughter Harper, was a devoted grandmother to many grandchildren. (Supplied: Elli Wust)

While they still miss their Mum, the family ensures she is not forgotten.

"We have a picture of Nanny up in her room and often talk about her," said Elli, who has had two more children since her mother died.

"Our little symbol of Mum was a butterfly, that's what we released at her funeral, so every time now we see a butterfly, even my middle child … she says, 'Mum, there's Nanny'.

title: Love story: Love when you don't know how long you've got
text:
When Kerrie Franc's daughter sleeps in some mornings, she worries she might "walk into the room and find she's not breathing." Kerrie's 14-year-old daughter Pippa has Williams syndrome. It is characterised by medical problems, developmental delays and learning challenges, and distinctive facial features. Despite being in a "constant state of anxiety" for Pip's wellbeing, 47-year-old Kerrie from Ballarat says life with her is full of joy.

Whether it's romantic, platonic, heartbreaking or surprising, our Love Story series is about love in all its forms. Share yours with us.

Pip stopped growing at 28 weeks — she had intrauterine growth restriction.

My obstetrician didn't really know why, and she was born at 38 weeks, but at 2 pounds 7 ounces (1.1 kilograms).

Tiny little tot.

Her lungs collapsed, so we were taken to Melbourne for quite a while until she was strong enough to come back to Ballarat.

We spent the first three months in and out of hospital. She was failure to thrive and there were feeding complications.

I kept on saying to the doctor, "There is something not quite right. She's not like my first daughter."

They said "No, she's fine, it's you, you have postnatal depression."

So they bundled me off to the mother-baby unit for two months.

The nurses usually take them for the first few nights to let you get sleep, but they ended up taking her full-time overnight as they had never had a kid that cried as much as Pippa.

We left and I was still saying something is wrong, but my concerns were dismissed.

Kerrie says Pippa is a sweet, loving child. (Supplied)

Finally, a paediatrician agreed to do some genetic testing. And at 15 months, Pip was diagnosed with Williams syndrome.

I was angry. I was really angry they wouldn't take my word that she was different to my first child.

And not just my word, but that she was failure to thrive, still tiny, wouldn't eat, wouldn't stop crying, wasn't meeting development milestones.

Masses of joy and love

There was no joy at the beginning of Pip's life.

She'd sleep for probably two hours in a row. My husband and I would take turns rocking her in one of those old-school rockers throughout the night.

And there was so much guilt. I had her big sister Tahlia, who was three, and I was trying to be there for her while also look after this kid who didn't stop crying.

It just all felt like I was never good enough, never quite there enough for everybody in the family.

It's still hectic today, there's not a lot of sleep.

I'm like a helicopter mum — always watching her — because there is no concept of danger. And she just wants to please people, so is very compliant, to a fault.

But there's masses of joy and love.

Williams syndrome awareness Morgan says she often has to explain her condition to support workers and teachers, so she's using social media to raise awareness of how it affects her everyday life.

Pip is developmentally about four years old, so we're always going to have the tooth fairy and Santa and the Easter Bunny.

All these magical things you get when kids are little. But I've got them forever.

When she does reach a milestone or do something new, you know how hard it has been for her.

And how long it has taken for her to achieve these things, like standing, walking, talking.

It's all that much sweeter when it happens.

She is such a loving, sweet kid. She's kind and compassionate and loves music and is always singing.

Kerrie says things were very difficult at the start of Pippa's life. (Supplied)

Of course, it's not always rainbows and sunshine.

She gets frustrated and jealous, and all her emotions are magnified.

But I wouldn't change it.

A future without my daughter

I fully expect one day to walk into her room one morning and find she's not breathing.

It's really quite difficult.

I've got peers around me who have lost their children. And like for me, their children were their world

There's the grief of 'What am I going do when she's gone?'

I have given my life to her forever. I've got no super, I've got no continuous employment. I'm a 47-year-old woman who has been out of the workforce for nearly 17 years.

What's my life going to be like without her?

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

On the flip side, if she survives until adulthood, what is my life going to be like and what is her life going be like when I'm gone?

I don't want her to be a burden on her sister, but I don't want to put her in a group home, either.

She's so innocent.

The future either way is full of heartache. But you just deal with it because she's my baby.

She will always be baby.

She will always be my little one who needs her mum to make the sandwiches and get her dressed and showered.

She may be a woman in body, but she loves The Wiggles and Santa, and that joy overrides all the worry.

Kerrie is an advocate with Carers Australia.

title: Yaraka publican shares why she's selling the town's only pub
text:
When Gerry Gimblett finished her term as a school principal in the heart of Queensland, she never thought she would end up back in town as the publican.

For 14 years, she and her husband kept the doors of the Yaraka Hotel open, after a chance phone call from the previous owners inspired them to buy the pub.

"It was like jumping off a cliff," Ms Gimblett said, laughing.

The town of Yaraka is home to about 20 people. (Supplied: Shel Turley )

Nestled in a sheep district on the "outer Barcoo", Yaraka's only pub captured global attention when it served a naughty mob of feathered customers a lifetime ban.

In 2020, the hotel was forced to barricade its entrance to keep Kevin and Carol, the emus, out after they developed a taste for tap beer and began targeting tourists.

Emus banned from outback pub The owner of the Yaraka Hotel says he doesn't really want the birds' patronage, because "when they get in, they behave a bit badly."

But last year, everything changed when Chris Gimblett died in a car crash that Ms Gimblett survived.

A 'new start' and legacy

Ms Gimblett said owning the pub had been an unexpected and deeply rewarding experience, but one she had always shared alongside her husband.

Chris and Gerry Gimblett with their son, Nick. (Australian Story: Brigid Donovan)

A year on from losing him, she said the time had come to sell.

"The ownership of the hotel was something that Chris and I did together, and it was very much a lifestyle option for us," she said.

The Yaraka Hotel was built in the early 20th century. (ABC Rural: Crispian Yeomans )

Ms Gimblett said mourning her husband had been difficult because she did not experience the cycle of grief she expected.

"He hit his head very, very hard and died immediately," she said.

"I was in rehab in Brisbane and thought to myself, 'Girl, you haven't really grieved.'

" I thought I would grieve when I got home, but when I went home, I had such a wonderful sense of peace. "

Standing on the balcony of the Yaraka Hotel as the sun melted behind the Messa Hills and ranges, she said she could feel her husband watching over her, "from the balcony of Heaven".

Gerry Gimblett says she feels her husband with her. (ABC Rural: Crispian Yeomans)

Looking for the 'right person'

The hotel is more than 100 years old, built when the town of Yaraka took shape in 1917 with the arrival of a railway.

Yaraka became the "end of the line" in western Queensland, but the station closed in 2005.

Yaraka is about two hours from Longreach. (ABC Western Qld: Craig Fitzsimmons)

For more than a century, the small pub has been the meeting place for locals who would go to town to collect supplies and mail from the train twice a week.

The pub has also attracted the likes of country music singers Graham Rodger and Lee Kernaghan, as well as travellers from across the country pulling up for the night.

Beyond the bar, the Gimbletts would invite patrons to climb on board a bus to watch the sunset from atop the red-top ranges on the outskirts of town.

Ms Gimblett said the trip was a "pay-what-you-like" activity that kept tourists in the town longer and fundraised for the Royal Flying Doctor Service.

Yaraka is near the popular tourist destination of Mount Slowcombe. (Supplied: Yaraka Hotel)

A fireplace the couple installed when they first bought the hotel was another way they extended hospitality to local graziers and caravaners passing through.

"To see people sitting around the fireplace and … the stresses and strains of city living, and you literally just watch it fall off," Ms Gimblett said.

Yaraka grazier Susan Glasson, who has lived in the area for 64 years, said she hoped the pub would continue as the "meeting place" of the town.

"We've had some longstanding great publicans at the hotel, and Chris and Gerry had been there for 14 years," Ms Glasson said.

"They've been very passionate and hospitable, and they've kept it going under some extreme circumstances."

Gerry Gimblett has run the Yaraka Hotel for 14 years. (Supplied: Yaraka Hotel)

Ms Gimblett said she wanted to sell to someone who understood the important role the pub played in the community, for local graziers and tourists alike.

"I don't want somebody to buy it as an investment because it's not that sort of business," she said, adding that owning the pub had been a "privilege".

"It's a very, very special community and that's why I'm choosing to stay to live here because I really want to remain part of it."

title: Fans left 'uncomfortable' as referee criticised in bloody heavyweight brawl
text:
Daniel Dubois came back from two knockdowns to deal Fabio Wardley a brutal and bloody first defeat as a professional and take the WBO heavyweight title in a thunderous all-British clash on Saturday.

Referee Howard Foster belatedly stepped in at the start of the 11th round to signal the end of the fight with Wardley bleeding heavily from the bridge of the nose and with his right eye almost closed.

"It was a war. We came through the sticky moments. Thank you Fabio for that," said Dubois, who was previously IBF champion after the belt was vacated by Oleksandr Usyk in 2024, with the Ukrainian winning it back in July 2025.

"What a great fight. What a great battle, man".

The win was Dubois's 23rd as a professional in 26 fights while Wardley now has a 20-1-1 record.

Veteran promoter Frank Warren, who manages both men, said it was the best heavyweight fight he had ever put on and confirmed there was a rematch clause in the contract.

For some viewers, however, it was also an uncomfortable watch that could have been stopped earlier.

Fabio Wardley (left) was brutalised in his world heavyweight title defence. (Getty Images: PA Images/Richard Sellers)

"I think it was uncomfortable at times," Richie Woodhall, former WBC super-middleweight world champion told the BBC.

"We know how tough Wardley is. We always said going into this, he was probably in with the hardest puncher he's faced. I can't believe the shots that he took, he never hit the canvas.

"The referee's let that go on too long. Fabio is a victim of that."

Veteran boxing reporter Steve Bunce noted that Wardley's corner should probably have taken him out of the fight in the ninth.

"Wardley has never been hurt like this," Bunce said.

"There is a case here for some mercy in the corner, there's a case for withdrawal.

"I know he's tough but there are limits. His corner looks very unhappy, they know what is imminent."

Daniel Dubois is now a two-time world heavyweight champion. (Getty Images: Matt McNulty)

The 31-year-old Wardley, who beat Australian Justis Huni by a 10th-round KO in June last year despite being comprehensively outboxed throughout, was promoted to WBO champion last November after Usyk vacated the title.

This was the Suffolk fighter's first defence and he once again showed immense heart as he took a tremendous beating yet refused to capitulate.

He had his opponent on the floor, a blow Dubois, 28, later dismissed as a "flash knockdown", almost with the opening bell still sounding.

Dubois was back on one knee in round three but came close to a stoppage in the sixth with the reigning champion bleeding heavily and on the ropes.

The Londoner continued to land blow after blow on Wardley, who wobbled but refused to give up.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Perhaps with that 10th-round KO against Huni in the back of their mind, Wardley's corner inspected the facial wounds after the eighth and doctors and the referee took a look in rounds nine and 10 but still the fight continued, with Wardley increasingly struggling to stay on his feet and fighting on instinct.

"You witnessed something special tonight," Warren told the BBC.

"Two men baring their hearts and souls in the ring, gave everything, didn't leave one bit outside the ring.

"They were getting hit with bombs that would take people out and they stood it.

"Fights like this, thankfully as a punter you like to see them but these are going right to their soul, they gave everything. The determination, the will to win, neither of them taking a backwards step. Amazing."

Reuters/ABC

title: Alexa Leary misses out on Commonwealth Games after reclassification
text:
Two-time Paralympic gold medallist Alexa Leary will miss this year's Commonwealth Games in Glasgow after the International Paralympic Committee adjusted her classification.

Leary, 24, was expecting to make her Commonwealth Games debut in Scotland.

The reigning world record holder in the S9 category has been reclassified as an S10 athlete, a faster classification for athletes with less-severe impairments.

Due to a limited program of events at the Commonwealth Games, there are no sprint freestyle events for S10 swimmers, with the only event in that category the 200 metre individual medley, which Leary does not compete in.

Leary has fast become one of the stars of the Australian Dolphins, with her effervescent personality belying a cycling accident that almost cost her life as a 19-year-old.

A budding triathlete, Leary spent six months in hospital after crashing at 75 kilometres per hour during a training ride on the Sunshine Coast in 2021.

Paralympic champion opens up on emotional volatility after TBI Two-time Paralympic gold medallist Alexa Leary is calling for a day to recognise traumatic brain injury and the unique challenges it brings.

Leary landed on her head, with surgeons having to remove part of her skull to save her life.

She also broke several bones and punctured a lung.

After taking up swimming again with an acquired brain injury, Leary became the darling of Australian swimming when she qualified for the Paris Paralympics, melting hearts with her emotional reaction to qualifying at the team trials in Brisbane.

Despite losing the three-time world champion and her infectious enthusiasm, Australia has named an 18-strong team for the Glasgow event, including seven debutants.

Defending 100m backstroke S9 gold medallist Timothy Hodge and 100m butterfly S10 champion Col Pearse headline the squad, which also features three-time Paralympic champion Lakeisha Patterson.

Lakeisha Patterson already has two Commonwealth Games gold medals to her name. (Getty Images: Quinn Rooney)

"It's always an honour to wear the green and gold and representing Australia at my fourth Commonwealth Games is special," said Patterson, who has won two Commonwealth Games golds in her lengthy and hugely successful career.

"The Commonwealth Games is the only time on the international stage we get to compete alongside our able-bodied friends, and we have more classifications on offer this time around.

"And I am so excited to have [14-year-old] Mia Hogan on the team, who is basically the same age as me when I made my Australian debut [15]."

To date, Hogan is the youngest member of the Australian Commonwealth Games team.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"I am so excited to join the Dolphins at the Commonwealth Games, to wear the green and gold, it's something I've dreamed about," Hogan said.

"I've learned so much from the more experienced swimmers … I'm really looking forward to joining them on team and racing on such a big stage."

Mel Tantrum, Swimming Australia's national head coach of the Paralympic program, said she was excited about the balance of the team.

"There is so much experience and youth in this team … from Tim, Col and Lucky through to Mia and all our debutants and the likes of Kirralee Hayes returning to the team — her first Australian team since Birmingham," Tantrum said.

"For us we're expecting a full medal sweep in some events and across the board we are expecting to be a very competitive team."

The rest of Australia's Commonwealth Games team will be announced at the conclusion of the Australian swimming trials in Sydney on 13 June.

title: From buckin' bulls to boxing: Aussie champ's new challenge
text:
Troy Dunn sits on the back verandah of his north Queensland cattle property, overlooking a valley of sugarcane, reflecting on his earliest days mounting up.

As a nine-year-old, he rode his first calf in a rodeo at Capella in central Queensland.

"He threw me over his head. I connected with his head and took a bit of bark off my cheek, and I thought, 'That was a good way to have fun,'" he said.

Dunn and his three brothers practised in between jobs on the family cattle property at Sarina, south of Mackay.

"We were bucking them in the fog some mornings at 6.30 before breakfast. That's where it all started," he said.

While feeding the dogs after a particularly good practice session, he decided he might be skilled enough to make a living from bull riding.

"That was my idea … to be able to get out of the chores," he said.

More than 20 years after retirement, Troy Dunn is still the best bull rider to come out of Australia. (Supplied)

Career highs

By the time he was 20, Dunn was riding professionally. Within two years, he won his first Australian title.

Highlights include riding Chainsaw, the legendary Brahman-cross that was named Australian Bucking Bull of the Year for eight consecutive years.

Canada and the USA beckoned and Dunn climbed the rodeo ranks there to ride the feared Red Wolf, a bull so wild it was involved in the death of rider Brent Thurman in 1994.

He recalls visiting Red Wolf years later and, although calm with tourists, the beast began huffing and scratching the ground when Dunn approached.

"He knew me. We were two-all, so we didn't have anything up on each other," he said.

The Golden Buckle awarded for Dunn's 1998 world championship. (ABC Tropical North: Liam McNally)

Dunn was at the top of the competition leaderboard in 1998 when he competed in the Professional Bull Riders (PBR) World Finals in Las Vegas.

Despite being thrown off in the first round and stomped by the bull, dislocating his hip, he was crowned World Champion. He remains the only Australian to do so.

"That was pretty amazing," he said.

Troy Dunn rode notorious bull Red Wolf four times in his career. (Supplied)

The next contender

Brady Fielder from Clermont in central Queensland, hopes to join Dunn as the second Australian to win the world title at the finals in Fort Worth, Texas, from May 7-17.

The 26-year-old is Australia's best current performer on the PBR circuit and is ranked second on the leaderboard.

Fielder is Australia's current top rider in the PBR. (Supplied: Professional Bull Riders)

"It has been an awesome season, it has been a heck of a journey and I'm thankful for the ending that I got. I'm feeling good going into the finals," Fielder said.

"It'd be a dream come true for me to be the second Australian to ever win that."

He said Dunn's "words of wisdom" had stuck with him.

"The will and the want to win … never let up on it and you'll get it eventually," Fielder said.

Brady Fielder is from Clermont in Central Queensland. (Supplied: Bull Stock Media)

A new ring

Despite suffering dislocations, concussions and a ruptured gut from a horn, Dunn said he never really got nervous before a competition.

"I wasn't worried about it, it was just something I loved so much," he said.

What does make Dunn nervous is boxing, a sport he has resumed after a few bouts as a younger man.

He got into it with friends to support a mate going through a rough patch.

"It was a one-for-all and all-for-one-type thing," Dunn said.

After retiring from bull riding, Dunn got back into boxing. (Supplied)

He's competed in more than a dozen Master's division bouts in the past eight years, and won silver at the Masters Boxing International World Titles.

"Getting in the ring, for me, takes that anxiety to a whole new level," he said.

"The day of the fight, you've just got this sick feeling in your gut all day and you can't eat.

"Getting on bulls never bothered me that way."

He said boxing kept him healthy and was easier on the body.

"You're not getting knocked around, you're fighting someone pretty much the same weight and age and experience," he said.

"You're not getting your limbs jumped on."

Dunn rode his last bull in 2005 and said he didn't miss the stress of constantly wanting to win.

Troy Dunn grew up around central and north Queensland. ((ABC Tropical North: Liam McNally))

He now runs riding clinics and encourages the next generation of "sticky lads" to chase their dreams if they think they have what it takes.

"Do it with all your might," he said.

"You don't have to be a world-beater … it's the journey that matters.

"[But] if you feel that you're gonna be a world-beater, go at it with everything you've got. Put it all on the line. You've only got one shot, you've only got one life, so make it count."

title: Chalmers reveals gas tax revenue is up in federal budget
text:
Treasurer Jim Chalmers has revealed the tax on offshore gas projects has raised more revenue than expected this year, as he prepares to hand down a budget focused on fixing a "broken" housing market and tax system.

Mr Chalmers is widely expected to break an election promise and overhaul the 50 per cent capital gains tax discount and negative gearing in Tuesday night's budget, which he admitted contained "a lot of political risk".

"The status quo in the housing market and in the tax system is broken," he told the ABC.

"It's not working. It is locking out too many Australians and we're not building enough homes."

Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson said the changes "would be very hard" for the Coalition to support based on the information that was available.

"The government is just taking from young Australians and from older Australians and not giving them anything in return. It’s very hard to see how you could back that," he told ABC Insiders.

Seeking to pre-emptively justify a broken promise, Mr Chalmers said Labor was focused solely on supply when it ruled out changes to negative gearing a year ago but was now turning its attention to other perceived barriers to home ownership.

Mr Chalmers has not ruled out sparing existing investors, and new builds, from the changes to negative gearing, referring to "transitional arrangements" when asked whether he would grandfather the more than 1 million Australians who currently take advantage of the tax break.

"When any government is considering changes of this nature, obviously, they work through the transitional arrangements and the transitional considerations," he said.

"I think any responsible government going down that path would do that."

The budget will include another $2 billion to build infrastructure including water, power and sewerage for housing developments, which the government says will unlock 65,000 new homes over the next decade. Labor is behind on its target of building 1.2 million homes by 2029.

Among the tax changes to be unveiled, the government will also make the $20,000 instant asset write-off for small businesses permanent.

Gas tax revised up

In the lead-up to the budget, the government had examined options for a windfall tax on gas giants and changes to the petroleum resource rent tax (PRRT), but the prime minister killed off the move, concerned it could upset the trading partners Australia is relying on for fuel.

However, Labor insiders concede the "gas tax" campaign is cutting through in the community and, last week, a cross-party senate committee urged the government to revisit the issue once the conflict in the Middle East had concluded.

The PRRT will raise more revenue than previously forecast. (Supplied: Woodside Energy)

One Nation leader Pauline Hanson has also picked up on the campaign, proposing an equity scheme in which the federal government would take an ownership stake in multi-billion-dollar projects, and funnel the revenue raised into a sovereign fund.

"What I want is a system where we will own part of that equity share in the exploration of gas, so we are not just going to allow them to take it," Senator Hanson said.

Mr Chalmers said the government was already taking "big and meaningful steps" in the gas market, pointing to a new east coast reservation policy unveiled last week, and that it was not proposing any changes to the existing tax arrangements.

"There's a lot of very strong views about it. I do understand the arguments that people are making," he said.

"I did change the PRRT in our first term to get more revenue sooner.

"There will be a bit of an upgrade to PRRT revenue in the budget on Tuesday night.

"I know that people would like us to go further but there are good reasons to prioritise fuel supply and gas reservation."

The PRRT raised $1.42 billion in 2024/25 and in December was forecast to generate $1.5 billion this financial year.

Reflecting on One Nation's win in Saturday's Farrer by-election, Mr Chalmers said while his budget was not a "political document", it would respond to growing concerns held by disaffected voters.

"It will take into consideration and respond to what I think are very real concerns that people have about our economy and our society," he said.

"This government doesn't lightly dismiss or deny the very real concerns that people have, which are encouraging them to consider political alternatives."

title: Students chasing hydrogen racing world title in Switzerland
text:
The Steel City was heralded as the Hydrogen Hub before the Whyalla Steelworks was forced into administration.

But now a group of high school students are taking Whyalla's hydrogen potential to the world stage.

For 17-year-old aspiring engineer Simon Coppins, it is a chance to see two years of "blood, sweat and tears" pay off.

Lead racer Simon Coppins aspires to be an engineer. (Supplied: Simon Coppins)

The H2 Grand Prix tasks competitors with building a hydrogen-powered RC car and completing the greatest number of laps in four hours.

According to team manager Caelan Kaminski, consistent lap times are the key to success.

"It's always about pacing yourself. We don't want to be the fastest car out there, because the fastest cars always run out of fuel the quickest," he said.

Team manager Caelan Kaminski. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

At the South Australian world qualifying event in 2025, the team was in first place by a heavy margin, but 30 minutes before the end, they were disqualified over a technical fault.

For lead driver Simon and his team, which had spent hundreds of hours perfecting their car, the outcome was heartbreaking.

The Whyalla team has spent hours building and working on its cars. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

"I just spent three hours and 30 minutes driving without blinking," he said.

"We were so close to our end goal."

According to race officials, the mistake came down to a simple misunderstanding, with the team swapping out one of its batteries during a mandatory maintenance check.

The RC cars are fuelled by hydrogen that the team produces. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

The team, which had established a dominating lead, did not need to make the switch and had they not done so, would have taken out first place.

Though they missed out on competing in Germany in 2025, fortune has given them another shot this year.

The race changed its scheduling format, hosting its world competition earlier in the year to better align with the northern hemisphere's calendar.

Going forward, qualifying events for countries in the Asia Pacific will be held the year before.

But as this decision was only made recently, qualifiers for the Switzerland competition could not be held in Australia.

Eli Coppins, Caelan Kaminski, Simon Coppins, Shannon Bayogos and Karman Randhawa practise ahead of their race in Switzerland. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

According to the organisers, several high-performing teams have subsequently been invited to the June meet, including Whyalla, as the team came so close to victory in 2025.

"It's definitely a big opportunity for all of us to prove Whyalla's worth and, you know, Whyalla's skills and community on a world stage," Caelan said.

The students have spent hours tinkering with their model cars to get them race ready. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

Sunrise Christian School science, technology, engineering, and mathematics teacher Trudi Wynn could not be prouder of how her students acted.

"They were very, very gracious in the disqualification," she said.

"It was obviously very disappointing.

"They were interacting with our sponsors and thanking them and apologising because they didn't obviously win.

"I think that they are an excellent representation of Whyalla."

Teacher Trudi Wynn praised the team for handling past setbacks with grace. (ABC News: Arj Ganesan)

Ms Wynn said the competition was a fantastic tool to inspire students to study STEM.

"This is why I'm a teacher," she said.

"My father was an inventor, so I'm very much into problem-solving and solving real-world problems and getting my students involved."

The team now needs to raise roughly $100,000 to cover the costs of return flights, accommodation, and equipment for the five students and five accompanying adults.

title: When the post office threatened to shut, locals found an unusual solution
text:
When the town's post office and general store announced it would be closing at short notice eight years ago, Clackline residents had one month to come up with a solution before services ceased.

The Wheatbelt town, 80km east of Perth, is home to just 330 people.

A small group of volunteers banded together to come up with a solution — transforming a vacant train carriage into a fully operational post office.

The former sleeper train carriage was transformed into the Clackline Post office in 2018. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

Former Clackline Progress Association president Michael Letch called a community meeting to discuss the issue in 2018, unsure if anyone would attend.

"When [my wife and I] rolled up to the meeting, we couldn't find a parking spot because there were 70 people there," he said.

Former Clackline Progress Association president Michael Letch spearheaded the post office relocation. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

"I don't think anyone took any notice of the unusual idea of the post office being in the carriage, as long as it was going to stay in Clackline.

"The whole community came together over that month to bring the idea to life."

Sleeper car transformed

Trains moved through Clackline to Northam and Toodyay until 1966, when a new rail route was opened.

Until its renovation, the carriage sat vacant on land across the road from the former general store.

The Clackline Post Office is a former sleeper train carriage that travelled through the town. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

Mr Letch said the new use of the train was fitting.

"The carriage was a sleeper car on the Kalgoorlie Express, and it used to thunder through Clackline," he said.

"In the earlier days, the to-ing and fro-ing of mail was by rail."

Community hub

Eight years on, the post office has become a community hub that includes a library and gardens, hosting daily catch-ups between locals.

The community garden at the Clackline Post Office was established by the late postmaster Laurie O'Neil. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

"With the loss of the school many years ago and the store, this has become a community centre," he said.

Clackline Post Office postmaster Carol O'Neil has been running the post office since the day it opened, motivated by the encouragement of her late husband, Laurie.

Mrs O'Neil said the connection the post office provided the community was vital, given its small population.

Clackline Post Office postmaster Carol O'Neil has worked in the carriage since its inception. (ABC Midwest & Wheatbelt: Eliza Bidstrup)

"We get mums who catch up out the front on their way home from dropping the kids off at the bus stop, people who are new to the town who get to be a part of something," she said.

"Before the post office was here, people would say hello in the street, but they didn't get the chance to know each other."

title: Victorian-era cider mill crushes first apples in more than 100 years
text:
When Braidwood carpenter Ned Bott was asked to refurbish a cider mill built by the Albert Day Foundry in Somerset in 1868, he had to do some serious guesswork about what the original looked like.

There are no simple instruction manuals online for such an old piece of machinery.

"We spent hours trying to find images of a similar type of mill, but to no avail," he said.

"So I had to work off what was there and recreate what I believed to be in existence at the time.

" Apart from the main timber frame, I had to rebuild all the timber parts and the hopper, the catch box, steel-toothed rollers, the roller box, and remade a few forged components, and wire-brushed for days trying to clean up the iron. "

Loading...

But with the old mill restored, he finally had a breakthrough that will allow him to put the finishing touches on it.

"In the last couple of weeks, we managed to get hold of someone who posted some photos of the same mill on a farm in Hestercombe [UK]. We got to find out a few more intricate parts we were missing, so there's a little bit more work to do on this," Mr Bott said.

First crush delights crowd

The cider mill had its first apple crush in more than 100 years on the weekend as part of a demonstration at the Braidwood Museum and Heritage Centre.

Not only did it perform well, but there are plans to use it regularly.

"I've reproduced this to be functional, and not just a museum piece," Mr Bott said.

"We'll use it next year, and I'll hopefully get the job to fix an apple press from the early 1900s, which is capable of pressing one tonne or more at any time, so if we get this going too, it'll be quite a sight."

Braidwood cider maker Gary Sully wants to use the cider mill to crush apples grafted from its original property. (ABC News: Floss Adams)

There are also plans to produce cider using apples grown on the very property where the mill was kept at Majors Creek, south of Braidwood.

"Many years ago, we grafted the root remnant trees of the Wilton Cider Factory, where the press and presser came from," Braidwood cider maker Gary Sully said.

" My dream is to get enough apples off the trees we've grafted and create the same cider they made in that day, using the same equipment. "

Mill was once breakthrough technology

The original Albert Day mill, pictured prior to its significant refurbishment. (Supplied: Gary Sully)

Mr Sully said this style of mill, known as an Ingenio mill, revolutionised cider-making when it was invented in the 1670s.

"Originally, the mills would've been stone troughs with a big stone wheel drawn around by a horse that crushed everything, including pips and stalks.

"This Ingenio mill was a watershed moment in cider making because it allowed a gap for the pips and stalks to go through the roller.

"Pips are full of arsenic, so they'll impart a bitter taste, and you get a rougher, unrefined drink."

Major refurbishment takes time

Mr Bott said it was about four weeks' worth of work, completed over the space of one and a half years.

The carpenter is used to being resourceful when it comes to machinery.

Ned Bott got interested in beer brewing during COVID lockdown and then switched to cider. (Supplied: Gary Sully)

During the COVID lockdown, he built his own cider mill using objects he found around his house and started making his own drinks.

"We'd collect apples from trees on the side of the road, and I'd get a workforce together and help make cider," he said.

"Then I got word there was an apple chitter at the museum that needed fixing up, and I was more than happy to take on the project."

title: Fix coming to mobile breast screening services, government says
text:
Angie Bisset had a mammogram booked on what is currently Tasmania's only mobile breast screening bus.

But the night before her long-awaited appointment, she received a call informing her the bus had "broken down" and would not be coming to Smithton in Tasmania's far north-west, where Ms Bisset lives.

She was given the option to re-book for June, which she took, conscious of the risks that could arise from delayed detection.

Angie Bisset says she is "close to the age" when her mum became sick. (Supplied: Angie Bisset)

Ms Bisset's mother died from breast cancer at age 43, something that weighs on her mind with every passing year.

"The fact that I'm close to the age when she got sick scares me so much," she said.

A young Angie Bisset (left) with her mother before she died of breast cancer. (Supplied: Angie Bisset)

Last year, Ms Bisset was not able to access the bus and had to travel from Smithton to Launceston — a 460-kilometre round trip — to have her mammogram, something she's not keen to do again amid rising fuel prices.

"I'm worried if this gets cancelled again, then I'm going to have no choice but to travel three hours just to get the scan done," she said.

" If I could, I would go private, but … I can't afford anything like that at the moment. "

Angie Bisset (right) and her mother (left). (Supplied: Angie Bisset)

Both BreastScreen buses affected by technical issues

The BreastScreen bus Luna, which serviced the state's south and the east coast, was taken off the road after water damage and technical issues caused two staff to receive electric shocks.

At the time, the Department of Health acknowledged "any reduction in screening rates increases the risk of women being diagnosed with breast cancer at later stages, when the cancer is more difficult and expensive to treat".

One of the Breastscreen units pictured in 2025. (Supplied: Kristie Johnston)

At the end of April, mammography equipment on BreastScreen Tasmania's bus Ida malfunctioned.

While the issue has been resolved and services have resumed, women like Ms Bisset, who have had to reschedule their appointments, have been left feeling anxious about the delays.

Leanne Roberson believes a BreastScreen Tasmania check saved her life. (Supplied: Leanne Roberson)

A potentially life-saving service

Leanne Roberson was 57 when the BreastScreen Tasmania bus made a stop in her hometown, which she believes saved her life.

"It was the last appointment of the day, and I hesitantly went because it was a cold, miserable day.

" I'm so glad I did, though, because … if I had waited 12 months, it might have been too late for me. "

Leanne Roberson is grateful to still be here to enjoy time with her family. (Supplied: Leanne Roberson)

The Ulverstone woman was diagnosed with stage 2 breast cancer and was able to have the tumour removed while retaining her breast.

When she learned about the situation at Smithton through a Facebook post, she felt compelled to share her own story, which was met with an overwhelmingly positive response.

"People want to have these checks done, and they can't access them," she said.

" I am so thankful to BreastScreen for catching my cancer early and allowing me to be here with my beautiful family and grandson. "

Labor's Anita Dow is calling on the government to improve access for regional women to BreastScreen services. (ABC News: Luke Bowden)

Braddon Labor MP Anita Dow said delays in accessing BreastScreen services showed the health of Tasmanian women in regional and remote communities was not being prioritised.

Ms Dow said the government had been slow to deliver its commitment to improving access to BreastScreen services.

"These are really important issues for regional Tasmanians who have to travel long distances to access specialist services and, quite rightly, they deserve commitments that have been made by the government to be delivered in a timely fashion," she said in parliament on Wednesday.

" Surely … access to BreastScreen services isn't too much to ask. "

Premier Jeremy Rockliff said "accessibility to women's health services is very important to me, it's very important to our team, and it's very important we get it right".

In a statement, a Department of Health spokesperson confirmed the screening equipment on board Ida "experienced technical issues, requiring replacement parts to be ordered from overseas as a priority".

Jeremy Rockliff says an effective breast screening service for women is "very important to me". (ABC News: Kate Nickels)

The issue has now been resolved, with Ida set to return to Smithton from May 18 to 22, then again from June 9 to 19 this year, they said.

"Clients who had their appointments postponed have had the opportunity to re-book when the unit returns. New clients are also being contacted to fill future appointment availabilities," the spokesperson said.

The new Devonport BreastScreen clinic is scheduled to open next month, and the department spokesperson said the delivery of a new mobile screening unit is "on track for completion later this year, with design and planning completed and construction to commence shortly".

The spokesperson said these projects, as well as the new Tasmanian Breast Care Centre at the Royal Hobart Hospital opening this month, will "provide a significant increase in access to breast screening for Tasmanian women".

The BreastScreen Tasmania Client Travel Subsidy is available to ensure Tasmanians can access breast screening services, as well as other community transport arrangements, the spokesperson said.

" BreastScreen Tasmania also helps to resolve any barriers for clients in accessing this important service at an individual level. "

title: The tax reform coming for Birkin bags, fancy watches and crypto
text:
Advertisement

Exclusive PoliticsFederalFederal budget The tax reform coming for Birkin bags, fancy watches and crypto Shane Wright May 10, 2026 — 1:55pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Investors with money in everything from cryptocurrencies to wine and even high-end handbags could be caught in planned changes to capital gains tax to be unveiled by Treasurer Jim Chalmers on budget night. As Chalmers promised the budget would include incentives for the start-up sector and venture capital, investors are increasingly concerned about how any change to CGT will affect returns on assets that have become increasingly attractive to younger Australians. The original Birkin bag created by Hermès in 1984, was recently sold at auction for $15.3 million. AP As this masthead revealed last month, Chalmers will return to the way capital gains were taxed before changes made by the Howard government in 1999. Before 1999, the value of assets was adjusted for actual inflation, with the CGT applied only to the “real” jump in value. This became a flat 50 per cent discount in a move then treasurer Peter Costello argued would make Australia more attractive to investors, particularly for the share market.

Advertisement

While most focus has been on shares and property, any change will affect new types of assets. The investment world has changed dramatically since the discount was introduced, with one of the largest being the advent of cryptocurrencies. The global market is valued at an estimated $US2.8 trillion ($3.7 trillion) with up to a quarter of Australian investors thought to hold crypto assets. Related Article Federal budget Chalmers ready to fight ‘scare campaign of lies’ before bravest budget yet Bitcoin, which accounts for more than half of all crypto value, has suffered a steep fall in price since late 2025, dropping from a peak of $US124,310 to around $US81,000 today. But an investor who has held Bitcoin since the start of 2024, when it was selling at $US44,000, is still sitting on an 85 per cent capital gain. There has also been an explosion in the luxury investment market since the turn of the century as investors sink cash into everything from fine wine to high-end watches and even Hermes’ signature Birkin handbags. In some cases, these purchases can attract CGT.

Advertisement

The secondary market for the Birkin has thrived in the last two decades, with some bags far more valuable second-hand than new, according to Knight Frank’s The Wealth Report 2025 report. The report shows handbags were the best-performing luxury asset class in 2024. Challenger Law managing director and principal lawyer, Tuan Van Le, said if changes to the CGT extended beyond property then it would likely reduce the incentive of investors to create their own crypto start-ups. He said many start-ups offered shares to their employees when establishing a business. The tax hit from the pre-1999 system was likely to be more than the current 50 per cent discount. “If the start-up is successful, they’ll end up paying more tax under the old system than with the discount,” he said. “It will be less enticing for people to start up their own crypto company.”

Advertisement

Van Le said changes to negative gearing, where the government is considering restricting the number of homes that can be negatively geared, could also encourage people to set up companies to invest in property. He said while companies cannot negatively gear, the lower tax rate offered under a company structure could encourage investors to structure their financial affairs as a company. Chartered Accountants ANZ group executive for policy, Geraldine Magarey, said the $500 threshold for assets that attract CGT had not changed since the tax was introduced, arguing it should be indexed. “Any change to the capital gains tax regime will require taxpayers to understand how the rules apply to them across a broad range of assets,” she said. “If you sell an asset after only a short period, inflation won’t have moved much, so indexation may give you a smaller benefit than the current discount. But if you hold an asset for many years, more of the gain is just inflation, so indexation can give long-term holders a fairer result.”

Advertisement

Tax counsel at The Tax Institute, John Storey, said ultimately crypto and other assets were the same as any other when it came to CGT. “There are a few specific quirks that affect those assets, but otherwise they are just taxed like any other investment,” he said. Related Article Federal budget Betting the house: Jim Chalmers’ big budget gamble “We don’t know how any 50 per cent CGT discount changes might affect crypto yet, but early indications are that any changes will apply across the board so crypto will be affected the same as other assets”. While Chalmers refused to be drawn on how the tax may be changed, he pushed back on suggestions it would hurt start-ups and venture capital.

Advertisement

He said the budget’s tax package would contain “difficult but necessary” reforms with a focus on helping young people get into the property market rather than targeting the investor sector. “I think what people will see in the budget on Tuesday is a lot of effort, including new policies, to support start-ups and venture capital. We see them as a really crucial part of the economy and increasingly so,” he told Sky News. “Without pre-empting any announcements in Tuesday night’s budget, there is a focus on start-ups and on venture capital, a positive one.” Cut through the noise of federal politics with news, views and expert analysis. Subscribers can sign up to our weekly Inside Politics newsletter.

title: As a Queenslander, Daly Cherry-Evans fears a James Tedesco Origin recall
text:
Advertisement

SportNRLNRL 2026 As a Queenslander, Daly Cherry-Evans fears a James Tedesco Origin recall Christian Nicolussi May 10, 2026 — 1:55pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

Daly Cherry-Evans fears James Tedesco will pose an even greater threat to Queensland if recalled by NSW – and it has nothing to do with what he is doing on the field. Cherry-Evans, now back in the Queensland Origin picture following Tom Dearden’s ankle injury over the weekend, knows that time away from the interstate series provides the hunger to prove that you should never have lost your spot in the first place. Loading And that is the predicament facing Tedesco, who has not played for NSW since game one of the 2024 series. Tedesco, 33, is in a shootout for the fullback spot with incumbent Dylan Edwards. Both players are in excellent form and have time to prove to coach Laurie Daley that they deserve the first shot at representing NSW in the May 27 series opener.

Advertisement

Following his Origin omission two years ago, Tedesco won last year’s Dally M Medal and has started this season in irresistible form. Cherry-Evans spent three years in Origin exile before he was recalled by Queensland, and quickly went about making the most of his second chance. Will James Tedesco be recalled by NSW? Getty Images He knows Tedesco will do likewise if given the chance to repay any faith shown by Daley and the selectors when the teams are named next Monday – and suggests that motivation gives him a mental edge over Edwards. “There’s always a level of hunger for the players who play rep footy; once you get a taste of it, you understand how great it is,” Cherry-Evans told this masthead.

Advertisement

“It makes you a better player, it challenges you. They are the things you want as a footy player. “My break from Origin was three years. It was a long time, I had a lot of time to work on my game and prove to selectors and coaches I was good enough to get back in there. Daly Cherry-Evans knows James Tedesco looms as an even greater threat to Queensland if recalled by NSW. Peter Rae “I’m sure James Tedesco has got the same level of hunger, if not more. “I haven’t spoken to him about it, but from afar, you can probably tell [how hungry he is]. If you want it bad enough, you work hard enough for it.

Advertisement

Advertisement

Meanwhile, the weekend ankle injury suffered by Tom Dearden means Queensland is suddenly in need of a halfback. Cherry-Evans and Roosters teammate Sam Walker are among the leading candidates. Like Tedesco, Cherry-Evans is closer to the end of his career than the start, but still playing a top-quality brand of football. “If you ask anyone playing in the NRL over 30, it’s [age] just a number,” Cherry-Evans said. “If Ted wasn’t my teammate, I’d feel a bit different about [a NSW recall]. But I’ve always been the same when it comes to teammates playing Origin; if they’re good enough and they get picked, I’m really happy for them because I know what that does for our team at the back end of the year with club footy.” Both Origin teams will be picked next Sunday night and announced on Monday after the completion of Magic Round.

title: BP secures 35-year Brisbane lease as state moves to secure fuel pipeline
text:
Advertisement

NationalQueenslandOil BP secures 35-year Brisbane lease as state moves to secure fuel pipeline Catherine Strohfeldt May 10, 2026 — 1:42pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

The state is rushing approvals for a decades-long lease of prime land near the Port of Brisbane for a global oil giant as part of the government’s plan to create a self-contained Queensland oil industry. Speaking on Sunday, Premier David Crisafulli said the government would promise 35 years of security to BP by renewing the global fuel company’s long-term lease on Bulwer Island, enabling it to begin work on upwards of 10 fuel storage tanks. “Our negotiations with BP had started earlier this year, before this [fuel] crisis, and we wanted to give them that lease certainty so they could invest here,” Crisafulli said. Premier David Crisafulli (right) speaks alongside BP’s Paul Auge at Bulwer Island on Sunday. Catherine Strohfeldt “That will enable them to refurbish five idle fuel storage tanks, and that will increase their capacity here by around 20 per cent – that’s a big play.”

Advertisement

Related Article Fossil fuels Brisbane diesel refinery receives $25 million boost from LNP The premier said the company also had plans to add another five tanks in the medium term, bringing the total capacity to 140 per cent of the current stores – or about 103 million litres. BP country president Paul Auge said it expected to spend $100 million restoring the five existing tanks – which were decommissioned in 2015 – with a view to reopening them by 2029. “We’ll start work next year; we’re doing the engineering now,” Auge said. “Obviously, with this news, we will look to accelerate that as much as possible.”

Advertisement

While the company did not have a timeline for the construction of five additional tanks, Auge said the oil giant has “options already that we can bring to the table to build more tanks”. “We’ve done a lot of work around the long-term strategy for these assets,” he said. The 10 tanks would store more than 100 million litres of diesel, gasoline and aviation fuel near the Port of Brisbane. Catherine Strohfeldt The premier said over that same medium- to long-term period, he wanted to open up more drilling and refining operations in Queensland, which he said would boost the state’s “sovereign capability” in the fuel space. “We got to store, we got to drill, we’ve got to refine, and we’ve got to control our own destiny again,” he said.

Advertisement

Advertisement

On Sunday, the premier also reiterated calls for the Albanese government to grant a national interest exemption for oil exploration, under its Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act, for the Taroom Trough project. Related Article Queensland votes Health at heart of Stafford byelection blitz “Until we have that dead hand of the EPBC removed, the ability to really get cracking in the Taroom Trough will be hindered,” Crisafulli said. When asked about the upcoming Stafford byelection, the premier refused to comment, but pointed to the government’s recently released ambulance ramping data. Queensland Primary Industries Minister Dale Last, however, said the byelection would be a referendum on Steven Miles’ leadership of the Labor Party.

Advertisement

Last said if there wasn’t a significant swing to Labor in the seat – which was held by Labor until former MP Jimmy Sullivan was removed from the party – the opposition leader was “toast”. “The pressure is back on Steven Miles. It is now up to him to deliver, and if he doesn’t, he’ll be walking a short plank,” Last said. Labor said voters were not interested in “mud slinging”, as it renewed calls for the state to restore beds it said had been cut at the Prince Charles Hospital. title: Waratahs had it all to play for. One part of their game was embarrassing
text:
Advertisement

SportRugby UnionSuper Rugby Opinion Waratahs had it all to play for. One part of their game was embarrassing Paul Cully Rugby columnist May 10, 2026 — 1:41pm

May 10, 2026 — 1:41pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

When another disappointing Waratahs season is dissected at the end of the campaign, the 31-26 loss to the Highlanders will be hard evidence of how much they have relied on warhorse captain Matt Philip. Without Philip, the Waratahs’ tight five looked big in stature but didn’t play big: a criticism that is starting to stick like mud to some of their individuals up front. Loading Their scrum was decimated, and when they gave up a soft maul turnover after 15 minutes, coach Dan McKellar looked like he was ready to explode. The Highlanders have an exceptional scrum, but their level of domination from the outset was borderline embarrassing for the Waratahs.

Advertisement

It can only be inferred that the Waratahs’ attitude wasn’t right, despite the fixture’s critical importance to their play-off hopes. It’s no guarantee Suaalii will start for the Wallabies If the Wallaby is going to shoot out of the defensive line, he needs to make those tackles stick. But he had bad misses on two players leading to the Highlanders’ first try, and the absence of Joey Walton in the midfield – another glue player like Philip – was keenly felt. Joseph-Aukuso Suaalii impressed in attack against the Highlanders, but made some costly defensive decisions. Getty Images The Suaalii scrutiny can be overboard, but he’s inviting it with plays like that. He certainly posted good attack statistics, but that was against a Highlanders team that played without a midfielder for 30 minutes.

Advertisement

The intelligent Six Nations teams arriving in July know how to attack that No.13 channel, so Suaalii’s defensive issues are a concern. The debate about his best position is firmly back on the table, with Hunter Paisami currently playing the house down at No.12 for the Reds and Len Ikitau being a natural No.13. At present, there is a scenario where Suaalii doesn’t necessarily start for Australia. Super Rugby Team of the Week 1. Tom Robertson (Force), 2. Lachlan Lonergan (Brumbies), 3. Apolosi Ranawai (Waratahs), 4. Seru Uru (Reds), 5. Lukhan Salakaia-Loto (Reds), 6. Jeremy Williams (Force), 7. Fraser McReight (Reds), 8. Harry Wilson (Reds), 9. Ryan Lonergan (Brumbies) - Player of the week, 10. Declan Meredith (Brumbies), 11. Lachie Anderson (Reds), 12. Hunter Paisami (Reds), 13. Josh Flook (Reds), 14. Zac Lomax (Force), 15. Max Jorgensen (Waratahs) Lomax impresses again – but there’s an asterisk attached Cross-code convert Zac Lomax will be a Wallaby in July at this rate of progress. He seems almost unnaturally at ease in the 15-man game.

Advertisement

While he potentially looked a bit narrow defensively on Brumbies winger Ollie Sapsford’s try in Canberra, that was more due to the quality of the pass from Brumbies halfback Ryan Lonergan. Lomax’s kick chase was outstanding again; even if he didn’t grab the Force’s box kicks, he did enough to cause issues for the Brumbies’ back three. Loading But almost paradoxically, his strength under the high ball is a bit of a trap. There were a few signs on Saturday that the Force were overdoing the tactic, particularly in the second half. They need to work hard to make sure they are pulling the trigger in attack rather than just falling for the default option of a kick for Lomax. It is a tricky balancing act, because Dylan Pietsch and Mac Grealy are on fire in the wider channels and need space. But as an individual making his first steps in rugby, Lomax is way ahead of schedule.

Advertisement

Brumbies win a game of two halfbacks Mark down the 14th-minute of the Force’s 32-15 loss to the Brumbies in Canberra on Saturday as the moment their play-off hopes were dashed. That’s when in-form halfback Henry Robertson was injured with what looked a relatively serious knee injury. Related Article Waratahs Too little, too late as Waratahs’ season slips away in Dunedin His replacement, Nathan Hastie, is an elite box-kicker and can attack with his running game, but he lacks Robertson’s overall snap. By contrast, Brumbies halfback Ryan Lonergan confirmed his standout display makes him the form Australian halfback in Super Rugby. The Brumbies’ game isn’t good enough to win the title, but Lonergan has arguably been the difference between them making the play-offs or not.

Advertisement

Wallabies captain back to his best The Reds will have legitimate grievances about the officiating in their 31-21 loss to the Chiefs in Brisbane; the normally mild-mannered Les Kiss was visibly incredulous at some of the decisions. But both Kiss and Wallabies coach Joe Schmidt will be delighted by Harry Wilson’s form at No 8. Wilson took the Chiefs on physically, and his offload to set up Joe Brial’s try was superb. Wilson’s form is critically important. Ireland No.8 and captain Caelan Doris missed the British and Irish Lions tour due to injury last year, but has been playing the house down for Leinster recently. The Wilson-Doris clash could be the most juicy individual match-up of the Wallabies’ July series. Watch every match of Super Rugby Pacific live and exclusive on Stan Sport.

title: From plain noodles to paella, this school is converting fussy eaters one lunch at a time
text:
Advertisement

NationalEducation From plain noodles to paella, this school is converting fussy eaters one lunch at a time Jackson Graham May 10, 2026 — 1:30pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

In a small school kitchen in Melbourne’s outer west, the smell of macaroni and cheese and steaming plates of paella fills the room as hungry students pile in. The children laugh at each other and dance; a typical scene of any canteen lunch line. Only this is Melton Specialist School – one of dozens across the state for students with intellectual disability, neurodiversity and physical disabilities – where a routine healthy lunch isn’t always guaranteed in a child’s school day. Simone Biancalani prepares food for about 100 students at Melton Specialist School but the kitchen is not big enough to expand the successful program. Joe Armao Simone Biancalani, the Tuscan chef behind the school’s newly launched lunch club, says his mac and cheese – which is packed with at least five kilograms of cauliflower – is a staple in the diets of 100 of its 400 students. Today’s paella, however, is a risky, unfamiliar dish the students have never encountered. “When I started, many wouldn’t even go close to the food,” Biancalani says. “It’s not about ego this job, it’s about trying to find the foods the kids enjoy.”

Advertisement

Lining up for lunch is Christian Cooke, a year 6 student with autism, ADHD, Klinefelter syndrome and mild intellectual disability. Before joining the program, he would eat only noodles and toasties for lunch. He often argued with his mother, Rachel, about the canned tuna or sandwiches she sent with him to school. Since eating Biancalani’s lunches, though, he can list at least eight new meals he enjoys – jacket potatoes are a clear favourite, as well as brownies made from sweet potato and chickpeas. Rachel Cooke with son Christian at the Melton Specialist School lunch club. Joe Armao “I wanted to eat it because the food is yummy,” Christian says. The program has been a circuit breaker at home, too. Where Christian’s diet was previously limited to plain dishes such as spaghetti cautiously dipped in sauce in a separate bowl, now he’ll eat nearly anything he’s tried at school.

Advertisement

“It’s because all the kids are eating together. The other kids were eating it, so he wanted to eat it, too,” Rachel says. Related Article Exclusive

Disability New NDIS eligibility tool will be ‘relatively blind’ to diagnoses: Butler Melton Specialist School principal Brooke Briody says the school environment removes a lot of the stress around food and mealtime. Melton Specialist School has been provided meals to some of its students, including Toby. Joe Armao “At home, there’s time pressure, there are costs, and that can add to the rigidity around their eating,” she says. “At school, they’re going to have fun regardless of whether they try a tiny bit or they have a lot.”

Advertisement

Australia is one of the few high-income countries without a national school meals program, according to an OzHarvest Report released last month. At Melton Specialist School, teachers are finding food is a direct opportunity for learning. Since beginning the trial last year, there has been a 13 per cent increase in students achieving social goals, and a 15 per cent increase in exceeding achievement goals. More than half of the students are attending school more often. “You can broaden your language just by being around food. And it’s an amazing motivator for some kids,” Briody says. After trying paella, Christian opted for some fruit instead. Joe Armao Despite the program’s success, much of the food comes from donations, and Biancalani is working in a classroom kitchen designed for students to learn food technology, not cater for hundreds of people.

Advertisement

Now, the school has plans to build a new kitchen and a bigger dining room that will allow them to feed all 400 students daily if it can fundraise $300,000 for a staged redevelopment of a communal area and unused classroom. “We can only have 10 or 12 kids eating together at one time. The social side of learning is really impacted by the space,” Briody says. After digging into the paella, Christian decides it isn’t for him, and politely asks for a bowl of fruit instead. Children learning to reject food and ask for something different is all part of the program, says school wellbeing leader Olivia Desormeaux. “When they go on excursions and family gatherings, they can say, ‘no, thank you’,” she says. “The learning here is empowering them.” Editor's pick Updated

AFL 2026 ‘Desperate for some relevance’: Brad Scott’s barb after GWS’ banner sledge targets Dons By the end of lunch, three-quarters of the students have tried the paella. Loved or loathed, the meal is a talking point teachers and carers can engage them with for the rest of the day.

Advertisement

“We aren’t saying we have all the answers, but we’re trying,” Desormeaux says. “And we are seeing a beautiful program get embedded in our school culture. Food is a real connector.” Start the day with a summary of the day’s most important and interesting stories, analysis and insights. Sign up for our Morning Edition newsletter.
